speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nison Katz. Tape number 4. October 2nd, 1995. So you've just gotten married. So I got married. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we lived still at the-- like, I call her grandmother. In her house, we had a little room. And we lived there for about two months or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found a job. So I got to make a living. She was working there. But it was still not enough. And so I got a job, and I start-- I start to work. And I been looking for a comfortable place. So she used to tell my wife that you don't look for anything. I'll find you some. And she did. She found us a house close to her house, what. We were living there till about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, about four, five, or six months, half a year or something. I don't know. But in the meantime, I did want to go home to Ostrag and to find out if there was somebody left from my-- at least from my relatives. Or if maybe somebody saw, saw someone from our family, or what. I got there and I found some people who I-- I knew them very well from before the-- from before the war. And I started to ask. I started to  ask gentiles. I mean, I ask-- they are neighbors, the gentiles, too, beside. And nobody have seen. One told me that the first day of the war, June the-- June the 21st, when the-- when the Germans got closer to our city and they started bombarding, my brother-in-law, Brucha's husband, he had some cattle. And he took the cattle out on the land to feed them. I don't know what, what he'd been doing there. And he got killed there, right away, from the bombardment there. That's what I-- they told me they know about it. They told us that they've seen my mother and my sister on the synagogue. They went to the synagogue. And that's all what I, I heard from them. And it was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of reaction did you get from these people? Were they happy to see you or not happy to see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, from the-- from the gentile people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you are talking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: How could they be happy? Because I knew them. It was neighbors. It was neighbors. And I knew them when I was little. I knew all around. It was-- [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was [NON-ENGLISH] and the second name, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a banderivtsi. He, he, he used to work Underground, for the banderivtsies. I mean, like the Ukrainian National Front. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, the people who survived the Holocaust, I mean, they're being around Austria. Well, there, there was a lot of them around Austria. They had been hiding in the woods. Then they went to the partisans, or what. So they knew everything about what was going on in the city. They told me that the, the-- [NON-ENGLISH], it was a neighbor of-- it was Jewish people. Well, the whole gang was Jewish, and especially these neighbors. The Jews, they had a little baby. He used to live there, the, the-- [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to live there and eat there, like a member of the family. And when the Germans got there, right in the beginning, he didn't recognize them. He didn't know them anymore. And he took a little baby-- a little baby, maybe, I don't know, six, seven weeks old-- and he threw it in the wall and killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- how do you know this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me-- the people. That's what I'm telling you. People, I mean, survived in, in, in, in the city-- not in the city, but around the city. So they knew. They, they got there-- they been there every day, almost-- not the day, at night. And they knew everything. So-- and they told me what's happened. Now, when, when I got married there and I went back a turn to find out if somebody left from, from my family, that's-- I, I knew what's happened. They told me what's, what's happened there. And they told me that Polish citizens can go to Poland-- I mean, a change there, exchange of citizens. Who was a Polish citizen can go to Poland. Who was a Russian citizen in Poland could go to Russia. Well, a lot of, of the people went to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told me that you can come over here. And you can do the same thing. They had registers. And you're going to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland, there was a transition. I mean, whoever had relatives in the States, or in Canada, or in Israel-- I mean, the, the priority to me, it was if I'll get to Poland, I'll-- going to go to Israel. That was-- my mind made up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there alone. My wife was in, in Kiev, in Rokytne there. So-- till I got there, and, and my wife was already pregnant. And we had a baby already. And there it was already 1947, or what. Till I took the baby with my wife from there-- This is your first son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My son. My oldest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tom-- Tevye.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his actual date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what, what what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 13th of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I-- till I got the baby with, with me and we went to Ostrog. They closed the exchange. And I got stuck there. I couldn't get out. I got stuck there for another 12 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrog. Another 12 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a foreman in a shop there, in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailoring?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a, yeah, in a tailor shop, or what. They used to work people. And I was a foreman. And I was a cutter there. I used to get the material and cut them. And I mean, like, what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there, established already. She had a job. She got a job, like, at-- Your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife had a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in a Dom-- in a culture house-- a Dom Kultury, or what, like the same things she saw during the-- she, she opening up courses. She taught you, I mean, cotton and sew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --or what. She had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And you also had another son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, not this side. I had a different factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. A son-- another son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There, we had another son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Tsvi

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and, and when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born when the-- April, April the 14th, 1950. He was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Ostrog until 1957? '59?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Ostrog until 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1957, I mean, I applied to get out of there. It was-- again, in 1955, like, it was-- people just had been talking. It's open again, the exchanges of citizens. But everybody was afraid. At that time, I was already there for 12 years already there, or what. And everybody was afraid to apply.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because if you apply and register to go out, they-- who knows? Because we was afraid. You didn't know with whom you're talking, or what. So I waited. I waited a year later. And then I decided, no, I got to register. And I registered. It took another-- till [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1957-- till 1957 until I got my visa. It took maybe a year and a half till I got the visa to, to get out from, from Russia to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I left, I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the president begged me. He, he, he cried, where you are going to Poland again-- again to, to Poland-- there is anti-Semitism. There is this. There is that. He didn't know what my mind was, or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, well, anyways, I left. We went-- we went out to Poland. We got-- I got-- we came on, on, on Liegnitz. First, I mean, first, we had been in repatration pools, I mean, like, you been a refugee-- refugee camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we left Russia, we went to Poland. But we were in refugee camps there for, I don't know, maybe more than a year. No, in the-- refugee camps, we been there a couple of months, not more. And then you got to decide where you want to go. So if you're decided where you're going to go-- I didn't know where to go. I, I knew some people went Liegnitz from our city. So I said we're going to Liegnitz. And we went-- I went back to Liegnitz there. We came in there. I mean, I have no job, She has no job. There was no, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no-- we haven't got money to make a living, or what. It took us a little bit. And I start to work for myself. I mean, I used to cut some clothing, like coats, and something like this, and used to sell it. I mean, not sell it. I used to give it-- it was a-- it used to called a commission store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to give it to the store. And they used to sell it. And I gave them for, for their work for they are selling it, and the rest, or what. So I start to making some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I find some work. I mean, like a contractor. I used to take out work at home. I used to work at home. And she used to work, again, there in the Dom Kultury, like, like from the old-- they opened up courses. She used to work there. So it was-- there, it was all right. And in the meantime, I did want to register to go to, to Israel. Her sister-- the older sister-- was already in Canada, or what, because she left-- she came to Canada in 1948, if I'm not mistaken. She was in Canada. The other sister was in Israel-- left. So and I did want to go to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they made up the-- their mind for me. They told us that you have to go to Canada, not to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish authorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say 'they,' who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, like your sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Your sisters. Why did they say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a meeting between themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why not go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- why not? Because it was in 1956, '57-- I mean, this was the war. It still-- it wasn't any work there. It was very bad, or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad. So, in the meantime, they, they made up our mind for us that we had to go to Canada. In Canada, we'll find-- I mean, there's lots of work you can find there. And you can establish yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the older sister made the papers. They sent us papers to Poland to take us in, I mean. And it didn't take long. We had a positive answer that we were accepted.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go to checkup, to Warsaw, to the Canadian consulate and to the Polish consulate. So we got to the Canadian consulate. We had an examination. And so I couldn't-- I didn't make it. I got-- I mean, I-- and I tell you I didn't get sick. I didn't know I was-- I was healthy. And they found something in the x-ray that the-- like tuberculosis, or something like that. So they didn't let me out. They had the visas to go. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I couldn't. They didn't allow me to go. So I had been stuck there for another two years. And so I went for another inspection.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did your-- did your-- but your wife stayed with you, in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and the kids stayed with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --or what. They stayed with me. They said they didn't want to leave me, or what. So we stayed another two years there. And then I was-- I went for a checkup again. I mean, here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had seen the pictures here, in Canada. I, I mean, like, the doctors from the consulate. My sister start to work to get some doctors from here-- and to check what is wrong. And they find there's nothing wrong with me. It showed-- it showed on the-- in the xray-- maybe when I was a kid, I had something, and it shows up-- and it showed up. So then they let-- they gave me the visa. And I came here as a-- as an immigrant, not as a landed immigrant. But until I here, they gave me a landed immigrant. During the here, I had to go to the Red Cross clinic for checkups over here. And then they gave me the landed immigrant after a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you been doing here, since you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I I, I got here. So they found me a job. But I start to work right away. I mean, like-- she had a cousin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hailey. And he had been working at Jack [? Parsland's ?] factory as a shipper there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was a cutter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he got me the job. So he got me the job right away because, you see, you couldn't get into Canada if you haven't got a job. So he got me the job, and I couldn't get out from Poland. So the job been waiting. I mean, they had been waiting-- he was keeping it. They didn't want to wait so long. But they liked him very much. So they kept the place for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got here, I went right away to the job and start to work there, or what. So I had already a job. And my, my wife, she hadn't got a job there, or what. And when I used to come back home from work in the evening, she used to cry the whole evening and the whole night for a month-- more than a month. I mean, you see, it-- in my case, it was already the third time-- one from Kiev to Ostrog, from Ostrog to Poland, from Poland here. Yeah, and it didn't take a month. It takes years. So in the meantime, I got established. So I got to sell everything to break everything, and to start here from scratch. So it, it was very bad. I mean, not that I'm complaining. I mean, when we got here, I mean, we lived with, with her sister in the beginning. She took us in, or what. But I had a job. So I made $0.80 an hour. I made-- during the week, I made $20 or $25 a week. I took all the money what I make, and I give it to the sister. Because, I mean, a family with two kids, or what, they got to support us, or what. So I did not think for, for money-- what, what a dollar is. I got paid, I bought it in, and I gave it to the sister-- to the sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived there for three months or so. And then we find a place further on, and moved out. And we moved in the-- in-- at Grey Street on a flat. What-- one room with a kitchen, and we paid $60 a month, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the problem was there-- it was nice people. But I had two kids. They had some friends, what. We are at work, the friends came to there. And they didn't like it. They, they didn't tell me. They called my sister-in-law to tell them that I had to move out because of the kids having friends coming at, at the house. So I moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where shall I move? I moved back into my sister-in-law, back there. I paid her the $60 a month. At least I was there-- she didn't complain about the kids, or what. After a couple of months, we moved. We find, again, a, a place. And we moved out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved out to Arlington, or what. There, we had two rooms with a kitchen. That wasn't bad. But it wasn't Jewish people. It was Italian people there, in this house. And from there, we start to look-- it was far. I mean, I worked at Spadina. I haven't got a car. And from there to go to work, the transportation wasn't so good. So I used to spend two hours to get to work and from work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to look for a closer place. So we went to look at Bathurst. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] asked some $80 a month, or something like that. And we couldn't afford it. So we started to talk with our sister-in-law. And she says, why are you going to pay $80 for a month? Look for a-- for a house-- to buy a house. So, so we did, what. Instead of paying $80-- we'll pay $80. We'll pay our own house. So we moved into Jane and Sheppard. It was a new development. And they had been selling the houses very low, or what, a down payment of a thousand-- $2000. I could give a down payment and move in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but the problem was at Jane, it was just a lot-- development. It's like a desert. There is no transportation, nothing. But I have not-- I haven't got any alternative, or what. So I bought the house there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it used to take me two hours in the morning to get to work, two hours in the evening to get to work. Sunday, Saturday, there were buses that didn't run, or what.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to walk to the bus stop. It was maybe a mile to the bus stop, or what. So but-- it was all, all right. I added-- it cost me about $80, $85 a month. I had-- I paid principal and interest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I paid it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have grandchildren, too, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two-- which son-- from which son? How, how many have which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my youngest son has three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll take them. We'll film them after.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my oldest has one girl, or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Mr. Katz, I just wanted to ask you, what-- how do you feel when you think back on those times? How do you feel about the, the Poles today, the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think-- what do you think about the German people today and the Poles? When you think back on those times, nowadays, how do you feel about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wouldn't go to back to Poland-- back-- never there. Imagine, after the war-- during the war, when we're-- when, when the Germans runned back, my friend-- he is in the picture, J.D. Katz-- he was lieutenant in the-- in the army. And he got into-- with his company into Ostrog. And they killed him. This was after the war already, or what. So I wouldn't go-- I wouldn't go for, for no money there. The Germans-- well, you can't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can't judge everyone the same. There is different people, different. Maybe they're pretending to be, what, but they are different. Like, I was in Germany for two years. Since '44 up to '46, I was in Germany. And I had established there, too, a factory. And Germans used to work for me there. Some of them were OK. Some of them, they took me up to their house. And I don't know. By chance-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by chance, the owner from the house left pictures on the table. And I happened to look them over. And I've seen what they sent from the front, how they killed the Jews on the pictures. It was their pictures on the-- on, on, on the table. And I couldn't take them because I couldn't steal them, or what. But it was on the table. So I, I, I could imagine what kind of people-- what they, they, they were, or what. But you can't judge everyone on the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some was nice people. Some people were even-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so you want me to finish? I'm finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finish your sentence. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or what? Some, some people were good. They even-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] about those people there, even though they were Germans, they kept some people-- Jewish people on the lagers. As a matter of fact, during the, the period when I was in Poland, or what, I-- being in the army, we went out in the army. And it was there three couples walking on the street. And they recognized me, or I recognized them. They are Jews. And we start to talk to them. And they told us they had been hidden on a tree-- on a tree in the, the first time. They used to go out at the evening to get some-- this is during the summertime. During the wintertime, they couldn't go out on the street because they'll be-- they'll be marks from the foot. So they didn't go out. They went out during-- when it used to snow. Then they go out to get some-- get some food.
